coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen storage performance of Ti–V-based BCC phase alloys with various Fe content
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, X.B.
Feng, S.L.
Wu, Z.
Xia, B.J.
Xu, N.X.
coredata.dc:description: The effect of Fe content on hydrogen storage characteristics of Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe (x =0, 2, 3, 4, 5) alloys has been investigated at 353K. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the alloys present BCC and C14 two-phase structures for all of the Fe-containing alloys. With the increasing Fe content, the lattice parameters of the BCC phase decrease, which results in an increase of the hydrogen desorption plateau pressure of the alloys. Among the studied alloys, Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe alloy exhibits the smallest PCT plateau slope and a more suitable plateau pressure (0.1MPa< P equ <1MPa). The maximum and effective capacities of the alloy are 3.32wt.% and 2.26wt.%, respectively, which are higher than other reported Fe-containing BCC phase alloys. In addition, the V/Fe ratio in this alloy is close to that of (VFe) alloy, whose cost is much lower than that of pure V.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage
Ti–V-based BCC alloys
Phase structure

Dependence of plateau slope upon various x for Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe alloys.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The hydrogen absorption–desorption temperature of the presented alloys is under 373K.

row.entry: Lattice parameters of C14 Laves phase (nm)

article.ce:title: Hydrogen storage performance of Ti–V-based BCC phase alloys with various Fe content

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lab of Energy Science and Technology, Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 200050, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effect of Fe content on hydrogen storage characteristics of Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe (x =0, 2, 3, 4, 5) alloys has been investigated at 353K. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the alloys present BCC and C14 two-phase structures for all of the Fe-containing alloys. With the increasing Fe content, the lattice parameters of the BCC phase decrease, which results in an increase of the hydrogen desorption plateau pressure of the alloys. Among the studied alloys, Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe alloy exhibits the smallest PCT plateau slope and a more suitable plateau pressure (0.1MPa< P equ <1MPa). The maximum and effective capacities of the alloy are 3.32wt.% and 2.26wt.%, respectively, which are higher than other reported Fe-containing BCC phase alloys. In addition, the V/Fe ratio in this alloy is close to that of (VFe) alloy, whose cost is much lower than that of pure V.

ce:section.ce:para: Suitable means for hydrogen storage are one of the key requirements for hydrogen fuel cell technology and for a possible future hydrogen economy in general. There are three major competing technologies for hydrogen storage: compressed gas cylinders, liquid hydrogen tanks and metal hydrides. Even though considered to be quite simple and inexpensive, the high pressure involved in hydrogen gas cylinders raise safety concern. Compressed hydrogen gas technologies also suffer from low volumetric densities. The liquid hydrogen tank for its part offers much greater storage capacity by weight and volume but there is also safety issues associated with the handling of cryogenic liquids and the problem of evaporation loss. Because of the low pressures involved in metal hydride technologies and the fact that the release of hydrogen takes place via an endothermic process, this method of hydrogen storage is the safest of all. Moreover, the hydrogen released from a metal hydride is of very high purity and therefore, can be used directly to feed a fuel cell. It seems therefore, that metal hydrides are suitable for the practical application for onboard hydrogen storage systems in fuel cell powered vehicles in the future.

So far, most of the hydrogen storage alloys are developed practically such as LaNi5, TiFe and TiMn2 [1–3]. However, the hydrogen storage capacities of these alloys are less than 2wt.%, which is insufficient for using in fuel cell powered vehicles. In recent years, the study of Ti-based BCC phase alloys has been promoted promptly in several laboratories due to their remarkable hydrogen capacities [4–7]. However, the lower hydrogen desorption capacity and higher cost are two critical drawbacks limiting their successful practical applications. In order to resolve the problems, much work has been done on improvement of the hydrogen storage characteristics and decrease of the cost for these alloys. Fe or V–Fe alloy, which have low price compared with that of pure V, was introduced to Ti-based BCC phase alloys in order to reduce the content of pure V in the alloys. Cho et al. [8] had investigated the effect of Fe addition on the hydrogen storage property of Ti0.16Zr0.05Cr0.22V0.57 alloy. However, the hydrogen absorption capacity of the alloy decreases from initial 3.55wt.% to about 2.0wt.% after a small quantity of addition of Fe (5at.%). Seo et al. also reported that Fe addition to V0.68Ti0.2Cr0.12 alloy results in an obvious decrease of hydrogen absorption capacity [9]. Table 1 presents main features of some typical Ti-based BCC phase hydrogen storage alloys. The desorption capacities of these Fe-containing alloys are less than 1wt.%.

In our previous study, the Ti–10Cr–18Mn–32V alloy presented higher hydrogen storage capacities, higher reaction rate with hydrogen and flatter hydrogen desorption-desorption plateau [13]. However, the plateau pressure of the alloy is less than 0.1MPa even at 353K, which is so low that most absorbed hydrogen cannot be released from the hydrides at moderate temperature and pressure.

In this study, the effect of Fe addition on hydrogen storage characteristics of Ti–10Cr–18Mn–32V alloy has been studied. The atomic radium of Fe is smaller than that of Ti, Mn, Cr and V. Therefore, it was expected that the Fe addition would reduce the lattice parameters and thus increase the plateau pressure of the present alloy. A decrease of the cost of the alloy by Fe addition is another goal.

The Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe alloy samples were prepared by magnetic levitation melting. A 50g ingot was turned over and remelted four times to insure its homogeneity. The as-cast ingot was crushed into powders with average particle size under 400μm (using for activation and PCT measurement) and 30μm (for XRD measurement), respectively. The crystal structures and lattice parameters were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation. Diffraction profiles were analyzed by the Rietveld refinement program RIETAN-97beta. The microstructures were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

Hydrogen absorption and desorption behaviors of hydrogen storage alloys were quantitatively characterized by the pressure–composition–temperature (PCT) curves, which were measured by using a Sieverts-type Gas Reaction Controller (GRC), a product of Advanced Materials Corporation (USA). GRC admits a controlled amount of hydrogen gas into the reaction chamber that holds a specimen and monitors the pressure of the gas while maintaining the temperature of the chamber. The amount of hydrogen absorbed by the specimen is determined by calculating the amount of the remaining gas. The GRC also allows for the study of the reverse process, namely, desorption of hydrogen. All of our hydrogen absorption processes were performed at 293K. When the absorption process was completed, the temperature of reaction chamber was set to a specified value and the chamber was gradually evacuated below 0.003MPa for the following desorption process. In this study, the maximum hydrogen capacity is defined as the amount of hydrogen absorbed at an equilibrium pressure of 3MPa at 293K and the effective hydrogen capacity is defined as the mount of hydrogen desorbed when the hydrogen pressure is decrease to 0.003MPa from 3MPa at 353K. The hydrogen content of the hydrogenated samples was controlled by introducing various hydrogen pressures into the sample reaction chamber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe content on phase formation
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns (Fig. 1 ) reveal that Ti–10Cr–18Mn–32V alloy shows single BCC structure, while the alloys with x =2, 3, 4 and 5 consist of a mixture of a BCC solid solution and the C14 Laves phase. The content of the C14 Laves phase in the alloys increases with x from 2 to 3, but drops from x =3 to 5. The calculated lattice parameters of each phase are presented in Fig. 2 and Table 2 . The lattice parameter a of the BCC phase decreases with the increasing x (Fig. 2(a)), which could be attributed to the smaller atomic radius of Fe than that of Ti, Mn, Cr and V. However, for the Laves phase of the alloys, the lattice parameters a decreases from x =2 to x =3, but increases from x =3 to x =5, and the opposite result is observed for the lattice parameters c (Fig. 2(b)). It suggests that Fe addition leads to a significant change in the crystalline structure of the alloys and a further analysis about the effect of this on the hydrogen absorption–desorption performance of the alloys will be discussed later.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe alloys. Two-phase microstructures are observed for all Fe-containing samples. The content of the C14 Laves phase increases from x =2 to x =3, but decreases from x =3 to x =5. This is in good agreement with the results of the XRD analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe content on PCT characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: The PCT desorption curves of the Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe alloys are shown in Fig. 4 . The data showing the effect of the Fe content on the PCT properties are summarized in Table 3 . The maximum and effective hydrogen capacity both decrease from x =0 to x =3 but then, increase from x =3 to x =5 (Fig. 5 ), which is coincident with the change of the BCC phase in the alloys. It should be due to the fact that the BCC phase absorbs more hydrogen than the C14 Laves phase [13]. For the Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe alloy, the maximum and the effective capacities of the alloy are 3.32wt.% and 2.26wt.%, respectively, which are higher than other those of reported Fe-containing BCC phase alloys. In addition, the V/Fe ratio in this alloy is near that of (VFe) alloy, whose cost is greatly lower compared to that of pure V.

It is well known that the plateau characteristics of Ti–V-based alloys can be correlated with their lattice parameters [6,8]. However, it is always difficult to determine the plateau pressure and slope factor from the PCT curves. In this study, the plateau pressure (P equ) and the slope factor (S f) are defined as follows [13]:
P
equ
=
P 2
+
P 1
2
S f
= ln
P 2
P
1 1
where P 2 and P 1 are the pressure indicated by the arrows in Fig. 4.

Fig. 6 shows that the P equ increases with increasing Fe content. This can be interpreted in terms of difference of the lattice parameters of the BCC phase in the alloys. With increasing x, the lattice parameters of the BCC phase decrease (Table 2) and thus the cell volume and the interstitial hole size decrease, which leads to an increase of the plateau pressure.

It has been known that, in addition to the chemical energy effect, the strain energies may contribute to determining the plateau slope behavior [14]. To estimate the strain energies of each sites in the lattice, the hole radius of various interstitials must be known. However, it is very hard to calculate the hole radius of each site in the lattice as the alloy concentration varies because the compounds investigated in this work are multicomponent alloy systems. Hence, a qualitative estimate of the strain energy has been obtained from the X-ray diffraction results of the alloys as a function of element concentration. The lattice strain can be estimated from the full width half maximum (FWHM) of the X-ray diffraction peaks.

Figs. 7 and 8
shows the progressive change in the FWHM of (110) peaks (BCC phase) and c/a ratio (C14 Laves phase) as a function of x for the Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe. Because all XRD data were obtained from the same particle size distribution (30μm), the change in FWHM in Fig. 7 represents the variation in the lattice strain as a function of x. On comparison of Figs. 7 and 8, both the change in the lattice strain and the change in the degree of slope in Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe show the same trend with Fe content, increasing from x =2 to x =3, but decreasing from x =3 to x =5. A similar relationship can be also obtained in the variation in c/a versus x (Fig. 7). The c/a ratio at x =3 is 1.641, which is the closest to ideal C14 Laves phase structure in this case. (The ideal c/a ratio for the C14 structure is 1.663 [15]). At x =3, the lattice strain as well as the c/a ratio of the alloy exhibit a maximum value. This implies that the size difference between the various interstitial sites is maximized. Thus, there is a relative strongly sloping plateau in the PCT curve. The above results indicate that the slope behaviors in the Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe system can be closely correlated with the strain energy of interstitial site associated with both the lattice strain and the structure change. Similar results have been shown by Park et al. [16] for the Zr–Ti–M–Fe systems (M=Mn, Cr and V).

In order to understand what kinds of hydride phases form and how the formation of each phase contributes to the hydrogen absorption property, the XRD diagram of the hydrogenated Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe sample with various hydrogen content was investigated as shown in Fig. 9 and Table 4 . It was found that three kinds of hydrides were formed. Comparable with the as-cast sample, no new peaks appear for the first hydride phase (H1) with 1wt.% hydrogen, besides broadening and weakening in height of the peaks. It means that a distorted BCC structure is formed. This hydride phase is rather stable, so that the absorbed hydrogen cannot be fully released under the present experimental conditions (Fig. 4). The second hydride phase (H2) with 2.5wt.% hydrogen content contains three phases with BCC, FCC and Zr3V3O structures. This is different from the observations of Fe-free BCC phase alloys, in which only a deformed FCC structure was formed [13]. For the third hydride phase (H3) with a saturated hydrogen capacity, two phases appear corresponding to deformed FCC and Zr3V3O structures. Obviously, among the three hydride phases, the second hydride phase (H2) is desirable for obtaining flatter PCT plateau and larger hydrogen capacity.

The PCT results of Fig. 4 show that more than 1wt.% hydrogen can not be released from the hydrogenated alloy in the pressure range of 0 to 0.1MPa when the temperature is below 353K, which results from the formation of the first hydride phase. In order to understand the residual hydrogen (under 0.1MPa) captured in the alloy, TG measurements were performed. Fig. 10 and Table 5 present the results for the Ti–10Cr–18Mn–32V and Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe alloy samples, which absorb hydrogen at 293K under 3MPa for 1h and then desorb hydrogen at 353K under 0.1MPa for 30min. Upon heating, an apparent weight loss occurs for the two samples within the same temperature range examined. In the case of Ti–10Cr–18Mn–32V alloy, a sharp weight loss was observed at 520K. The terminal reaction temperature is about 745K. In the case of Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe alloy, the beginning and terminal reaction temperatures decrease to 460K and 530K, respectively. It implies that Fe addition decrease the stability of the hydrides, resulting in a more easy release.

The results obtained in this work are summarized as follows.
1.
In the Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe alloy system, the BCC phase content and the hydrogen storage capacities both decrease with x from 0 to 3, but increase with x from 3 to 5.
2.
The PCT plateau pressure of Ti–10Cr–18Mn–(32−x)V–xFe alloys increase with increasing Fe content, which results from the decrease of the lattice parameters a of the BCC phase.
3.
Three hydride phases are observed in the hydrogenated Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe alloy, differing from the Fe-free Ti–V-based BCC alloys.
4.
The Ti–10Cr–18Mn–27V–5Fe alloy exhibits a smaller slope and a better plateau pressure (0.1MPa< P equ <1MPa). The maximum and the effective capacities of the alloy are 3.32wt.% and 2.26wt.%, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Hi-Tech Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2003AA515022).
